narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 42.7% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is higher than what the school considers acceptable for admission. Your profile includes being ranked in the bottom decile for grades in your first year at school, the second-bottom decile in your third year, a middle-class family income, an LSAT score of 36, an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, identifying as male, White, and planning to attend full-time. Compared to typical students in our dataset—who often rank around the middle of their class in both early and later years, score about 36.7 on the LSAT, and have a 3.21 GPA—your academic rankings stand out as notably lower. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your third-year class rank, which pushed the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure because it signals weaker performance relative to peers. Next in importance was your first-year rank, also in the bottom decile, which similarly contributed to the failure risk by highlighting early struggles. Your middle family income followed closely, nudging the outlook slightly toward failure compared to higher-income peers who tend to pass more often. Your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA, both a bit below average, played supporting roles in elevating the risk, as did your racial background, gender, and full-time status, though these had minimal sway. Overall, these factors together painted a picture of elevated bar passage challenges for someone with your profile. While demographics like gender and race are fixed, you can realistically boost your future bar success by targeting academic weaknesses—such as retaking the LSAT for a higher score, building stronger study habits to improve GPA if you're still in undergrad, or seeking tutoring to climb class rankings in law school if admitted elsewhere. Many students turn things around by focusing on consistent performance and bar-specific prep courses early on. With targeted improvements, especially in grades and test scores, you could shift your predicted odds dramatically in future applications. Keep pushing forward—you've got the determination to outperform this prediction.
